content:
# Mark Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 33v–34v (PDF pages 66–68) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_005_page_001.jpg`, `MRK_005_page_002.jpg`, `MRK_005_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ They crossed the sea and landed in the region of the Gerasenes.

² The moment Jesus stepped out of the boat, a man came out of the tombs to meet him—a man controlled by an evil spirit.

³ This man lived among the burial caves. Nobody could restrain him anymore, not even with chains.

⁴ He had been chained hand and foot many times, but he always tore the chains apart and smashed the shackles. No one was strong enough to control him.

⁵ Day and night he wandered among the tombs and through the hills, screaming and slashing himself with sharp stones.

⁶ When he saw Jesus from far off, he ran to him and threw himself at his feet.

⁷ He shrieked at the top of his voice, "What do you want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? In God's name, don't torture me!"

⁸ For Jesus had already commanded, "Come out of this man, you evil spirit!"

⁹ Then Jesus asked him, "What is your name?" "My name is Legion," he answered, "because there are many of us."

¹⁰ And he begged Jesus again and again not to send them out of the area.

¹¹ A large herd of pigs was feeding on the nearby hillside.

¹² The spirits begged him, "Send us into the pigs! Let us go into them."

¹³ He let them go. The evil spirits came out of the man and entered the pigs, and the entire herd—about two thousand animals—stampeded down the cliff into the sea and drowned.

¹⁴ The men tending the pigs ran off and spread the news through the town and countryside. People poured out to see for themselves.

¹⁵ When they reached Jesus, they found the man who had been possessed by a legion of demons sitting there calmly, fully dressed, completely sane. And they were terrified.

¹⁶ Eyewitnesses told them what had happened to the demon-possessed man and about the pigs.

¹⁷ Then the people began begging Jesus to leave their region.

¹⁸ As Jesus was climbing into the boat, the man who had been set free begged to go with him.

¹⁹ Jesus wouldn't let him. Instead he said, "Go home to your family. Tell them everything the Lord has done for you and how he showed you mercy."

²⁰ So the man left and began telling his story all across the ten cities of the Decapolis. Everyone who heard it was amazed.

²¹ When Jesus had crossed back over the lake, a large crowd gathered around him at the shore.

²² A synagogue leader named Jairus arrived. The moment he saw Jesus, he collapsed at his feet

²³ and pleaded desperately, "My little girl is dying. Please—come and put your hands on her so she will be healed and live."

²⁴ Jesus went with him, and a massive crowd followed, pressing in on every side.

²⁵ In the crowd was a woman who had been bleeding for twelve years.

²⁶ She had been through agony at the hands of doctor after doctor. She had spent every penny she had, and after all that, she was only worse.

²⁷ She had heard about Jesus, and she pushed through the crowd from behind and touched his robe.

²⁸ She was thinking, "If I can just touch his clothes, I'll be healed."

²⁹ Instantly the bleeding stopped. She could feel it in her body—the disease was gone.

³⁰ At that same moment, Jesus felt healing power go out from him. He stopped in the middle of the crowd and asked, "Who touched my clothes?"

³¹ His disciples looked at him incredulously: "You can see the crowd crushing in on you from every side, and you ask, 'Who touched me?'"

³² But Jesus kept scanning the crowd, looking for the person who had done it.

³³ The woman, realizing what had happened to her, came forward shaking with fear and dropped to her knees before him. She told him the whole story.

³⁴ He said to her, "Daughter, your faith has healed you. Go in peace. Your suffering is over."

³⁵ While Jesus was still speaking, messengers arrived from Jairus's house. "Your daughter is dead," they said. "There's no point bothering the teacher now."

³⁶ Jesus overheard them and said to Jairus, "Don't be afraid. Just keep believing."

³⁷ He let no one follow him except Peter, James, and James's brother John.

³⁸ When they arrived at the synagogue leader's house, Jesus found chaos—people weeping and wailing.

³⁹ He went inside and said to them, "Why all this commotion and crying? The child isn't dead. She's sleeping."

⁴⁰ They laughed in his face. But he cleared them all out. Then, taking only the child's father and mother and his three disciples, he went into the room where the girl lay.

⁴¹ He took the girl's hand and said to her, "*Talitha koum!*"—which means, "Little girl, get up!"

⁴² Immediately the twelve-year-old girl stood up and started walking around. Her parents were absolutely stunned.

⁴³ Jesus gave them strict orders not to tell anyone what had happened, and then said, "Give her something to eat."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **The Gerasene demoniac — a portrait of total destruction (vv. 1-5):** Mark piles up details that any first-century hearer would recognize as the portrait of a man utterly destroyed: living among the dead, naked, self-harming, beyond human control. Then Mark contrasts this with the "after" picture in v. 15: sitting, clothed, in his right mind. The transformation is complete.

2. **"Legion" (v. 9):** The name is Roman military language—a legion was 5,000-6,000 soldiers. In occupied Palestine, this detail would carry unmistakable political resonance: the demons are an occupying force. Their drowning in the sea may echo the Exodus motif of enemy armies swallowed by water.

3. **"They were terrified" (v. 15):** The people are not grateful. They see the healed man and become afraid—not of the demons, but of Jesus. They beg him to leave. Mark is unflinching about the fact that the power of God can be more threatening than the power of evil.

4. **The sandwich structure (vv. 21-43):** Mark's signature literary technique is on full display here. He begins the Jairus story (vv. 21-24), interrupts it with the bleeding woman (vv. 25-34), then returns to Jairus (vv. 35-43). The two stories interpret each other: both feature "twelve years," both involve faith, both concern female bodies, and both move from desperation to wholeness.

5. **"Your suffering is over" (v. 34):** The Greek says "be healed from your affliction" (ἴσθι ὑγιὴς ἀπὸ τῆς μάστιγός σου). The word μάστιξ means "whip" or "scourge"—her disease is described as a scourging. I rendered the emotional release of that moment.

6. **"Talitha koum" (v. 41):** Aramaic preserved in the Greek manuscript—Jesus's actual words. The tenderness is unmistakable: he calls her "little girl" (ṭalyeṯā). This is the voice of someone who has entered a house of death and speaks life with gentle authority.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
